name: I'm pregnant and was exposed to chemicals at work in Ogden. What are my rights?
description: You are entitled to "reasonable accommodations" to move away from toxins. If your baby is harmed by the exposure, you may be able to sue your employer directly

mainEntity.name: I'm pregnant and was exposed to chemicals at work in Ogden. What are my rights?

acceptedAnswer.text: You are entitled to "reasonable accommodations" to move away from toxins. If your baby is harmed by the exposure, you may be able to sue your employer directly for damages, as the "exclusive remedy" of workers' comp often doesn't apply to the child.
acceptedAnswer.description:
If you are pregnant and exposed to chemicals at work in Ogden, your rights are protected under both workers' compensation and the Pregnancy Discrimination Act. If the chemicals are "teratogenic" (harmful to a fetus), your employer is required to provide "reasonable accommodations," such as moving you to a different department with better air quality.

If you or your baby are harmed, the legal situation is unique. While your injury is covered by workers' comp, a "birth defect" or injury to the child caused by the exposure is often NOT covered by workers' comp, meaning you can sue the employer for full civil damages on behalf of the child.

Key factors include the specific chemical's known effects on pregnancy and whether you requested a transfer. Many employers mistakenly think they can just "fire" a pregnant woman to avoid the liability of chemical exposure; this is highly illegal. You should consult a lawyer immediately to protect both your health and your baby's future. This is one of the few areas where an employer's "immunity" from lawsuits is often stripped away. Your child's rights are separate from your own.
